Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17100 (premium Rs. 141) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17100 (premium Rs. 111). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17352 | Lower BE: Rs. 16848
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17241 | Lower BE: Rs. 16989
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17402 | Lower BE: Rs. 16798
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17100 | Lower BE: Rs. 16848
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17352 | Lower BE: Rs. 16848
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 141 + 111 = Rs. 252. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17100 + 252 = Rs. 17352. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17100 - 252 = Rs. 16848.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17200 (premium Rs. 142) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17200 (premium Rs. 112). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17454 | Lower BE: Rs. 16946
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17342 | Lower BE: Rs. 17088
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17504 | Lower BE: Rs. 16896
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17200 | Lower BE: Rs. 16946
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17454 | Lower BE: Rs. 16946
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 142 + 112 = Rs. 254. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17200 + 254 = Rs. 17454. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17200 - 254 = Rs. 16946.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17300 (premium Rs. 143) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17300 (premium Rs. 113). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17556 | Lower BE: Rs. 17044
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17443 | Lower BE: Rs. 17187
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17606 | Lower BE: Rs. 16994
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17300 | Lower BE: Rs. 17044
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17556 | Lower BE: Rs. 17044
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 143 + 113 = Rs. 256. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17300 + 256 = Rs. 17556. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17300 - 256 = Rs. 17044.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17400 (premium Rs. 144) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17400 (premium Rs. 114). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17658 | Lower BE: Rs. 17142
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17544 | Lower BE: Rs. 17286
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17708 | Lower BE: Rs. 17092
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17400 | Lower BE: Rs. 17142
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17658 | Lower BE: Rs. 17142
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 144 + 114 = Rs. 258. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17400 + 258 = Rs. 17658. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17400 - 258 = Rs. 17142.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17500 (premium Rs. 145) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17500 (premium Rs. 115). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17760 | Lower BE: Rs. 17240
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17645 | Lower BE: Rs. 17385
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17810 | Lower BE: Rs. 17190
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17500 | Lower BE: Rs. 17240
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17760 | Lower BE: Rs. 17240
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 145 + 115 = Rs. 260. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17500 + 260 = Rs. 17760. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17500 - 260 = Rs. 17240.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17600 (premium Rs. 146) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17600 (premium Rs. 116). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17862 | Lower BE: Rs. 17338
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17746 | Lower BE: Rs. 17484
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17912 | Lower BE: Rs. 17288
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17600 | Lower BE: Rs. 17338
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17862 | Lower BE: Rs. 17338
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 146 + 116 = Rs. 262. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17600 + 262 = Rs. 17862. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17600 - 262 = Rs. 17338.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17700 (premium Rs. 147) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17700 (premium Rs. 117). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17964 | Lower BE: Rs. 17436
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17847 | Lower BE: Rs. 17583
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18014 | Lower BE: Rs. 17386
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17700 | Lower BE: Rs. 17436
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17964 | Lower BE: Rs. 17436
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 147 + 117 = Rs. 264. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17700 + 264 = Rs. 17964. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17700 - 264 = Rs. 17436.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17800 (premium Rs. 148) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17800 (premium Rs. 118). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18066 | Lower BE: Rs. 17534
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17948 | Lower BE: Rs. 17682
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18116 | Lower BE: Rs. 17484
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17800 | Lower BE: Rs. 17534
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18066 | Lower BE: Rs. 17534
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 148 + 118 = Rs. 266. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17800 + 266 = Rs. 18066. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17800 - 266 = Rs. 17534.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17900 (premium Rs. 149) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17900 (premium Rs. 119). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18168 | Lower BE: Rs. 17632
B. Upper BE: Rs. 18049 | Lower BE: Rs. 17781
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18218 | Lower BE: Rs. 17582
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17900 | Lower BE: Rs. 17632
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18168 | Lower BE: Rs. 17632
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 149 + 119 = Rs. 268. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17900 + 268 = Rs. 18168. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17900 - 268 = Rs. 17632.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 18000 (premium Rs. 150) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 18000 (premium Rs. 120). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18270 | Lower BE: Rs. 17730
B. Upper BE: Rs. 18150 | Lower BE: Rs. 17880
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18320 | Lower BE: Rs. 17680
D. Upper BE: Rs. 18000 | Lower BE: Rs. 17730
Test 6.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18270 | Lower BE: Rs. 17730
Test 6.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 150 + 120 = Rs. 270. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 18000 + 270 = Rs. 18270. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 18000 - 270 = Rs. 17730.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] Which of the following is responsible for guaranteeing the financial settlement of all trades executed on the equity derivatives segment of an exchange?
options: C. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI)
D. The Board of Directors of the Stock Exchange
Test 6.explanation: The Clearing Corporation acts as a central counterparty (CCP) and guarantees the settlement of all trades by novating the contracts.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] What is the consequence of placing an order on the exchange system at a price outside the operating range?
options: A. The order goes into a price freeze and requires exchange approval
B. The order is automatically cancelled
C. The order is executed at the market price
D. The trader's account is suspended
Test 6.answer: A. The order goes into a price freeze and requires exchange approval
Test 6.explanation: Orders outside the operating range trigger a price freeze and require verification by the exchange before being allowed into the system.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] Which of the following is correct regarding the accounting of equity derivatives by corporate companies?
options: A. Open interest positions must be marked-to-market and recognized in the balance sheet
B. Options premium paid is directly debited to the Share Capital account
C. Derivatives are always kept as off-balance sheet items and never reported
D. Only profits are recognized, losses are ignored
Test 6.answer: A. Open interest positions must be marked-to-market and recognized in the balance sheet
Test 6.explanation: According to Indian accounting standards, derivative contracts are financial instruments and must be recognized on the balance sheet at fair value.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] As per current SEBI guidelines, what is the maximum position limit for a mutual fund house in an index futures contract?
options: A. Higher of Rs. 500 crores or 15% of the total open interest of the market
B. Flat 5% of the market open interest
D. Mutual funds are not allowed to take positions in index futures
Test 6.answer: A. Higher of Rs. 500 crores or 15% of the total open interest of the market
Test 6.explanation: SEBI prescribes position limits for mutual funds as a whole, which is higher of Rs. 500 crores or 15% of the total open interest of the index.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] In option trading, what does the term 'implied volatility' (IV) represent?
options: A. The market's expectation of the future volatility of the underlying asset as reflected in the option price
B. The historical standard deviation of the asset's price returns
C. The tracking error of the index option
D. The speed at which delta decays
Test 6.answer: A. The market's expectation of the future volatility of the underlying asset as reflected in the option price
Test 6.explanation: Implied volatility is the volatility value that, when plugged into an option pricing model (like Black-Scholes), matches the current market price of the option.

Test 6.question: [Ref. Question] Advanced Concept Question Pool 65: Which of the following best describes the margin term 'Value at Risk' (VaR)?
options: A. A statistical measure of the maximum potential loss on a portfolio over a specific time horizon at a given confidence level
B. The absolute minimum cash margin a broker can accept
C. The total loss incurred on a default
D. The standard deviation of the risk-free rate
Test 6.answer: A. A statistical measure of the maximum potential loss on a portfolio over a specific time horizon at a given confidence level
Test 6.explanation: Value at Risk (VaR) measures the potential loss in value of a risky asset or portfolio over a defined period for a given confidence interval (typically 99% for exchange margins).

Test 6.question: Which of the following margins is collected on a gross basis across all clients of a trading member?
Test 6.explanation: Initial margin and extreme loss margin are collected on a gross basis at the client level, while MTM can be netted across positions of the same client.

Test 6.question: In the Black-Scholes model, which of the following parameters is NOT directly observable in the market?
Test 6.explanation: While spot, strike, interest rates, and time to expiry are known, volatility must be estimated or implied from option prices.

Test 6.question: An investor buys a stock at Rs. 500 and simultaneously buys a Put option of strike Rs. 480 for a premium of Rs. 15. What is the maximum loss the investor can suffer?
Test 6.explanation: The investor's downside is protected at 480. Maximum loss = Purchase Price (500) - Strike (480) + Premium Paid (15) = 20 + 15 = Rs. 35.

Test 6.question: What does the term 'Gamma' indicate in options trading?
options: Sensitivity of option premium to time decay
Rate of change of Delta with respect to the underlying asset's price
Change in option premium for a 1% change in volatility
Test 6.answer: Rate of change of Delta with respect to the underlying asset's price
Test 6.explanation: Gamma measures how much the delta of an option changes when the underlying asset's price changes by 1 unit.

Test 6.question: Which derivative strategy involves buying a call option and selling a call option with a higher strike price, both having the same expiration date?
Test 6.explanation: A bull call spread is created by buying a lower strike call and selling a higher strike call to lower the overall cost of the position.

Test 6.question: The daily settlement price of a futures contract is generally determined by:
options: The closing price of the underlying asset in the cash market
The opening price of the futures contract
The volume-weighted average price of the futures contract in the last 30 minutes of trading
The highest price of the day
Test 6.answer: The volume-weighted average price of the futures contract in the last 30 minutes of trading
Test 6.explanation: Exchanges typically use the VWAP of the last 30 minutes of trading to determine the daily settlement price for MTM purposes.

Test 6.question: If the calendar spread is created by buying a far month contract and selling a near month contract, the trader is likely expecting:
options: The near month contract to decay faster than the far month contract
Test 6.answer: The near month contract to decay faster than the far month contract
Test 6.explanation: Time value decays faster for near-term options. A calendar spread benefits from the accelerated time decay of the short, near-term option.

Test 6.question: What is a 'synthetic long futures' position?
options: Buying a Call and selling a Put with the same strike and expiry
Buying a Put and selling a Call with the same strike and expiry
Buying a Call and buying a Put with the same strike
Buying the stock and selling a Call
Test 6.answer: Buying a Call and selling a Put with the same strike and expiry
Test 6.explanation: Buying an ATM Call and selling an ATM Put creates a payoff profile identical to a long futures position.

Test 6.question: The process of replacing an expiring derivative contract with a new contract in a later month is known as:
Test 6.explanation: Rolling over a position involves closing out the near-month contract and simultaneously opening a position in a further-out month.

Test 6.question: According to SEBI regulations, client-level position limits in index derivatives are:
options: Not applicable; limits only apply to brokers
Restricted to 5% of the total open interest
Higher of Rs. 500 Crores or 15% of the total open interest in the market
Test 6.answer: Restricted to 5% of the total open interest
Test 6.explanation: For individual clients, the position limit is generally 5% of the total open interest in the market for index derivatives, whereas mutual funds/FPIs have the "higher of 500Cr or 15%" limit.

Test 7.question: Case: An index comprises three stocks: Stock A, Stock B, and Stock C. On January 1, 2024, their prices and shares outstanding were: Stock A: Rs. 100 (50 lakh shares), Stock B: Rs. 200 (30 lakh shares), Stock C: Rs. 150 (40 lakh shares). The base value of the index was set to 100 on January 1, 2024. On April 1, 2024, the prices changed to: Stock A: Rs. 120, Stock B: Rs. 210, Stock C: Rs. 160. The index is a market capitalization-weighted index. Calculate the market capitalization of the index on January 1, 2024.

Case: An index comprises three stocks: Stock A, Stock B, and Stock C. On January 1, 2024, their prices and shares outstanding were: Stock A: Rs. 100 (50 lakh shares), Stock B: Rs. 200 (30 lakh shares), Stock C: Rs. 150 (40 lakh shares). The base value of the index was set to 100 on January 1, 2024. On April 1, 2024, the prices changed to: Stock A: Rs. 120, Stock B: Rs. 210, Stock C: Rs. 160. The index is a market capitalization-weighted index. Calculate the new value of the index on April 1, 2024.

Case: A trader takes a long position in a Nifty futures contract on March 1, 2025, at Rs. 22,000. The lot size for Nifty futures is 25. The initial margin charged by the broker is 10% of the contract value. On March 2, 2025, the Nifty futures contract closes at Rs. 22,150. What is the total contract value of the trader's position?
Test 7.explanation: Contract Value = Futures Price * Lot Size = 22,000 * 25 = Rs. 5,50,000.

Test 7.question: Case: A trader takes a long position in a Nifty futures contract on March 1, 2025, at Rs. 22,000. The lot size for Nifty futures is 25. The initial margin charged by the broker is 10% of the contract value. On March 2, 2025, the Nifty futures contract closes at Rs. 22,150. What is the Mark-to-Market (MTM) gain for the trader on March 2, 2025?
Test 7.explanation: MTM Gain = (Closing Price - Trade Price) * Lot Size = (22,150 - 22,000) * 25 = 150 * 25 = Rs. 3,750.

Test 7.question: Case: The Nifty 50 index is currently trading at 20,500. A European call option on Nifty 50 with a strike price of 20,400 has a premium of Rs. 180. The lot size for Nifty options is 50. What is the intrinsic value of this Nifty call option?
Test 7.explanation: Intrinsic Value of Call Option = Max (0, Spot Price - Strike Price) = Max (0, 20,500 - 20,400) = Rs. 100.

Test 7.question: Case: The Nifty 50 index is currently trading at 20,500. A European call option on Nifty 50 with a strike price of 20,400 has a premium of Rs. 180. The lot size for Nifty options is 50. What is the time value of this Nifty call option?
Test 7.explanation: Time Value = Option Premium - Intrinsic Value = 180 - 100 = Rs. 80.

Test 7.question: Case: An investor plans to buy 1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. on July 15, 20XX, for which the current spot price is Rs. 500. To hedge against a potential price rise, the investor goes long one XYZ futures contract expiring on July 27, 20XX, at a price of Rs. 505. The lot size of the contract is 1,000 shares. On July 15, 20XX, the investor buys the shares in the spot market at Rs. 530, and the futures contract is trading at Rs. 532. What is the gain on the investor's futures position?
Test 7.explanation: Gain on Futures Position = (Futures Sale Price - Futures Purchase Price) * Lot Size = (532 - 505) * 1,000 = 27 * 1,000 = Rs. 27,000.

Test 7.question: Case: An investor plans to buy 1,000 shares of XYZ Ltd. on July 15, 20XX, for which the current spot price is Rs. 500. To hedge against a potential price rise, the investor goes long one XYZ futures contract expiring on July 27, 20XX, at a price of Rs. 505. The lot size of the contract is 1,000 shares. On July 15, 20XX, the investor buys the shares in the spot market at Rs. 530, and the futures contract is trading at Rs. 532. What is the net effective purchase price per share for the investor?
Test 7.explanation: Cost of Buying in Cash Market = 1,000 * 530 = Rs. 530,000. Net Cost = Cash Cost - Gain on Futures = 530,000 - 27,000 = Rs. 503,000. Net Cost per Share = 503,000 / 1,000 = Rs. 503.

Test 7.question: Case: The spot price of Stock B is Rs. 100. A one-month futures contract on Stock B is trading at Rs. 90. The contract multiplier is 200 shares. The arbitrageur can invest proceeds from cash market sales at 9% p.a. for one month. What is the immediate arbitrage profit per share from executing a reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage?
Test 7.explanation: Immediate arbitrage profit = Spot Price - Futures Price = 100 - 90 = Rs. 10 per share.

Test 7.question: Case: The spot price of Stock B is Rs. 100. A one-month futures contract on Stock B is trading at Rs. 90. The contract multiplier is 200 shares. The arbitrageur can invest proceeds from cash market sales at 9% p.a. for one month. If the arbitrageur carries the position for one month, what is the total gain from this arbitrage, including interest earned, assuming continuous compounding?

Case: An investor purchases a stock in the cash market at Rs. 1,600. To protect against a fall in price, the investor simultaneously buys a put option with a strike price of Rs. 1,600, paying a premium of Rs. 20. What is the maximum possible loss for the investor with this protective put strategy?
Test 7.explanation: The maximum loss for a protective put is the premium paid for the put option. In this case, Rs. 20.

Test 7.question: Case: An investor purchases a stock in the cash market at Rs. 1,600. To protect against a fall in price, the investor simultaneously buys a put option with a strike price of Rs. 1,600, paying a premium of Rs. 20. If the stock price rises to Rs. 1,660 at expiry, what is the net profit for the investor from this strategy?
Test 7.explanation: Profit on Long Cash = 1,660 - 1,600 = 60. Loss on Long Put = -20 (since it expires worthless). Net Profit = 60 - 20 = Rs. 40.

Test 7.question: Case: Clearing Member A has two Trading Members (TMs), PQR and XYZ. For the Nifty May contract, PQR has a proprietary net long position of 2,000 contracts, Client 1 (PQR) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (PQR) has a net long position of 2,000 contracts. TM XYZ has a proprietary net short position of 1,000 contracts, Client 1 (XYZ) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (XYZ) has a net short position of 1,000 contracts. What is the total long open position for TM PQR?
Test 7.explanation: PQR's Long Position = Proprietary Net Long (2,000) + Client 1 Net Long (1,000) + Client 2 Net Long (2,000) = 5,000 contracts.

Test 7.question: Case: Clearing Member A has two Trading Members (TMs), PQR and XYZ. For the Nifty May contract, PQR has a proprietary net long position of 2,000 contracts, Client 1 (PQR) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (PQR) has a net long position of 2,000 contracts. TM XYZ has a proprietary net short position of 1,000 contracts, Client 1 (XYZ) has a net long position of 1,000 contracts, and Client 2 (XYZ) has a net short position of 1,000 contracts. What is the total short open position for Clearing Member A?
Test 7.explanation: TM XYZ's Short Position = Proprietary Net Short (1,000) + Client 2 Net Short (1,000) = 2,000 contracts. TM PQR has no short position. Total Short Position for Clearing Member A = 0 + 2,000 = 2,000 contracts.

Test 7.question: Case: An investor sells an option in securities. The option premium is Rs. 50. Later, the option is exercised, and the settlement price is Rs. 1,500. What is the Securities Transaction Tax (STT) payable on the sale of this option?
Test 7.explanation: STT on sale of an option = 0.10% of option premium = 0.0010 * 50 = Rs. 0.05.

Test 7.question: Case: An investor sells an option in securities. The option premium is Rs. 50. Later, the option is exercised, and the settlement price is Rs. 1,500. What is the Securities Transaction Tax (STT) payable if this option is exercised?
Test 7.explanation: STT on sale of an option where option is exercised = 0.125% of the settlement price = 0.00125 * 1,500 = Rs. 1.875.

Test 7.question: Case: A portfolio consists of three stocks: Stock X, Stock Y, and Stock Z, with respective betas of 0.8, 1.2, and 1.5. The investment proportions (weights) in the portfolio are 40% for Stock X, 30% for Stock Y, and 30% for Stock Z. The market value of the portfolio is Rs. 1,00,00,000. The index futures price is 20,000, and the lot size is 50. What is the beta of this portfolio?

Case: A portfolio consists of three stocks: Stock X, Stock Y, and Stock Z, with respective betas of 0.8, 1.2, and 1.5. The investment proportions (weights) in the portfolio are 40% for Stock X, 30% for Stock Y, and 30% for Stock Z. The market value of the portfolio is Rs. 1,00,00,000. The index futures price is 20,000, and the lot size is 50. How many index futures contracts are required to hedge this portfolio's systematic risk (rounded to the nearest whole number)?
Test 7.explanation: Number of contracts = (Value of Portfolio * Portfolio Beta) / (Index Futures Level * Contract Multiplier) = (1,00,00,000 * 1.13) / (20,000 * 50) = 1,13,00,000 / 10,00,000 = 11.3 contracts. Rounded to the nearest whole number, this is 11 contracts.

Test 7.question: Which of the following option Greeks measures the sensitivity of an options price to changes in the implied volatility of the underlying asset?
Test 7.explanation: Vega measures the rate of change of the options value with respect to the volatility of the underlying asset.

Test 7.question: What does Theta represent in options pricing?
Test 7.explanation: Theta measures the rate of decline in the value of an option due to the passage of time.

Test 7.question: According to the Put-Call Parity theorem, which of the following equations holds true for European options?
Test 7.explanation: The put-call parity formula is C + PV(X) = P + S, where C is the call price, P is the put price, S is the spot price, and PV(X) is the present value of the strike.

Test 7.question: In the event of a stock split of ratio 2:1, how are the derivatives contracts adjusted?
options: Strike price is halved, lot size is doubled
Strike price is doubled, lot size is halved
Strike price is halved, lot size remains same
Test 7.answer: Strike price is halved, lot size is doubled
Test 7.explanation: To ensure the contract value remains the same, a 2:1 stock split will result in halving the strike price and doubling the market lot.

Test 7.question: Which of the following corporate actions typically does NOT require an adjustment to the strike price and lot size of derivative contracts?
options: Ordinary Dividend (less than 5% of market value)
Extraordinary Dividend (more than 5% of market value)
Test 7.answer: Ordinary Dividend (less than 5% of market value)
Test 7.explanation: Ordinary dividends (typically those below 5% of the market value of the underlying) are not adjusted in the derivatives market.

Test 7.question: Which entity sets the Initial Margin and Exposure Margin requirements in the Indian derivatives market?
Test 7.explanation: The Clearing Corporation computes and sets the margin requirements (like SPAN and Exposure margins) to manage counterparty risk.

Test 7.question: A trader goes long 1 lot of Nifty Futures at 22,000 (Lot size = 25). The closing price for the day is 22,100. What is the MTM settlement for the trader?
Test 7.explanation: MTM = (Closing Price - Buy Price) * Lot Size = (22,100 - 22,000) * 25 = +Rs. 2,500. The trader receives this amount.

Test 7.question: What is the primary purpose of a short hedge in the futures market?
options: To protect against an anticipated rise in the price of the underlying
To protect against an anticipated fall in the price of the underlying
To profit from a stagnant market
To increase the delta of the portfolio
Test 7.answer: To protect against an anticipated fall in the price of the underlying
Test 7.explanation: A short hedge involves selling futures to lock in a selling price, protecting an existing long position from a potential price decline.

Test 7.question: Which of the following best describes Open Interest?
options: Total number of contracts traded during the day
Total number of outstanding contracts that have not been settled or closed out
Total number of buyers in the market
The daily volume of the market
Test 7.answer: Total number of outstanding contracts that have not been settled or closed out
Test 7.explanation: Open interest represents the total number of active contracts held by market participants at the end of the day.

Test 7.question: If both a new buyer and a new seller initiate a trade to create a new contract, what happens to Open Interest (OI)?
Test 7.explanation: When a new long (buyer) and a new short (seller) enter the market, a new contract is created, and Open Interest increases by 1.

Test 7.question: What is the theoretical maximum profit for a buyer of a Put option?
Test 7.explanation: The maximum profit for a put buyer occurs if the underlying asset price drops to zero. Profit = Strike Price - 0 - Premium Paid.

Test 7.question: Which of the following options can only be exercised on the expiration date?
Test 7.explanation: European style options can only be exercised on the expiration date, unlike American options which can be exercised any time before expiration.

Test 7.question: When is a Call option considered to be "In-The-Money" (ITM)?
options: When implied volatility is very high
Test 7.explanation: A call option is ITM when the underlying asset's current spot price is greater than the strike price of the option.

Test 7.question: Which margin is intended to cover the largest possible loss that a portfolio may suffer in 99% of scenarios?
Test 7.explanation: Initial margin is computed using SPAN (Standard Portfolio Analysis of Risk) based on Value at Risk (VaR) to cover a 99% confidence interval of losses.

Test 7.question: The difference between the spot price of an asset and the futures price of that asset is known as:
Test 7.explanation: Basis is defined as the Spot price of the underlying asset minus the Futures price.

Test 7.question: If the futures price is higher than the spot price, the market is said to be in:
Test 7.explanation: A contango market implies that the futures price is higher than the current spot price, usually due to the cost of carry.

Test 7.question: Which Greek letter measures the sensitivity of an option's price to a change in the risk-free interest rate?
Test 7.explanation: Rho measures the expected change in an option's price for a 1% change in the risk-free interest rate.

Test 7.question: What kind of derivative is an agreement between two parties to exchange cash flows in the future based on a pre-arranged formula?
Test 7.explanation: A swap is a derivative contract through which two parties exchange financial instruments, such as cash flows or liabilities.

Test 7.question: A trader executes a Bear Put Spread. How is this strategy constructed?
options: Buy a higher strike Put and sell a lower strike Put
Buy a lower strike Put and sell a higher strike Put
Buy a higher strike Call and sell a lower strike Call
Sell a higher strike Put and sell a lower strike Put
Test 7.answer: Buy a higher strike Put and sell a lower strike Put
Test 7.explanation: A bear put spread involves buying a put option with a higher strike price and selling a put option with a lower strike price to reduce the cost.

Test 7.question: What does a high positive Delta (close to +1) signify for an option?
options: It is a deep OTM Call option
It is a deep ITM Call option
It is a deep OTM Put option
It is a deep ITM Put option
Test 7.answer: It is a deep ITM Call option
Test 7.explanation: A delta of +1 indicates that the option price moves practically point-for-point with the underlying asset, characteristic of a deep ITM Call option.

Test 7.question: In India, who regulates the commodity derivatives market?
options: Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
Test 7.answer: Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
Test 7.explanation: Following the merger of FMC with SEBI in 2015, SEBI regulates both the equity and commodity derivatives markets in India.

Test 7.question: For a physically settled stock derivative, what happens upon expiration if an ITM stock option is exercised?
options: Cash difference is paid by the seller
Delivery of the underlying shares must take place
The contract rolls over to the next month
Test 7.answer: Delivery of the underlying shares must take place
Test 7.explanation: Under physical settlement rules, exercising an ITM stock option requires the actual delivery (buying or selling) of the underlying shares.

Test 7.question: What is the 'Cost of Carry' model used for?
options: Determining the fair value of a Futures contract
Test 7.answer: Determining the fair value of a Futures contract
Test 7.explanation: The Cost of Carry model calculates the theoretical futures price based on the spot price plus the cost to carry the asset to expiration (interest, storage, minus dividends).

Test 7.question: What is an 'Option Writer'?
options: A person who buys an option
A person who sells an option
The exchange that creates the option
Test 7.answer: A person who sells an option
Test 7.explanation: The seller of an option contract is referred to as the option writer. The writer receives the premium and takes on the obligation.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] An ABC Call option with a strike price of Rs. 18100 has a premium of Rs. 105 and a Delta of 0.41. If the price of ABC stock increases by Rs. 50, what will be the new approximate premium of the Call option, holding other factors constant?
Test 7.explanation: New Premium = Old Premium + (Delta * Change in Underlying Price) = 105 + (0.41 * 50) = Rs. 125.5.

B. Upper BE: Rs. 16938 | Lower BE: Rs. 16692
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17096 | Lower BE: Rs. 16504
D. Upper BE: Rs. 16800 | Lower BE: Rs. 16554
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17046 | Lower BE: Rs. 16554
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 138 + 108 = Rs. 246. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 16800 + 246 = Rs. 17046. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 16800 - 246 = Rs. 16554.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 16900 (premium Rs. 139) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 16900 (premium Rs. 109). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17148 | Lower BE: Rs. 16652
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17039 | Lower BE: Rs. 16791
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17198 | Lower BE: Rs. 16602
D. Upper BE: Rs. 16900 | Lower BE: Rs. 16652
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17148 | Lower BE: Rs. 16652
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 139 + 109 = Rs. 248. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 16900 + 248 = Rs. 17148. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 16900 - 248 = Rs. 16652.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17000 (premium Rs. 140) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17000 (premium Rs. 110). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17250 | Lower BE: Rs. 16750
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17140 | Lower BE: Rs. 16890
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17300 | Lower BE: Rs. 16700
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17000 | Lower BE: Rs. 16750
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17250 | Lower BE: Rs. 16750
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 140 + 110 = Rs. 250. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17000 + 250 = Rs. 17250. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17000 - 250 = Rs. 16750.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17100 (premium Rs. 141) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17100 (premium Rs. 111). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17352 | Lower BE: Rs. 16848
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17241 | Lower BE: Rs. 16989
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17402 | Lower BE: Rs. 16798
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17100 | Lower BE: Rs. 16848
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17352 | Lower BE: Rs. 16848
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 141 + 111 = Rs. 252. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17100 + 252 = Rs. 17352. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17100 - 252 = Rs. 16848.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17200 (premium Rs. 142) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17200 (premium Rs. 112). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17454 | Lower BE: Rs. 16946
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17342 | Lower BE: Rs. 17088
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17504 | Lower BE: Rs. 16896
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17200 | Lower BE: Rs. 16946
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17454 | Lower BE: Rs. 16946
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 142 + 112 = Rs. 254. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17200 + 254 = Rs. 17454. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17200 - 254 = Rs. 16946.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17300 (premium Rs. 143) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17300 (premium Rs. 113). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17556 | Lower BE: Rs. 17044
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17443 | Lower BE: Rs. 17187
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17606 | Lower BE: Rs. 16994
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17300 | Lower BE: Rs. 17044
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17556 | Lower BE: Rs. 17044
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 143 + 113 = Rs. 256. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17300 + 256 = Rs. 17556. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17300 - 256 = Rs. 17044.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17400 (premium Rs. 144) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17400 (premium Rs. 114). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17658 | Lower BE: Rs. 17142
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17544 | Lower BE: Rs. 17286
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17708 | Lower BE: Rs. 17092
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17400 | Lower BE: Rs. 17142
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17658 | Lower BE: Rs. 17142
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 144 + 114 = Rs. 258. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17400 + 258 = Rs. 17658. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17400 - 258 = Rs. 17142.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17500 (premium Rs. 145) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17500 (premium Rs. 115). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17760 | Lower BE: Rs. 17240
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17645 | Lower BE: Rs. 17385
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17810 | Lower BE: Rs. 17190
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17500 | Lower BE: Rs. 17240
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17760 | Lower BE: Rs. 17240
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 145 + 115 = Rs. 260. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17500 + 260 = Rs. 17760. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17500 - 260 = Rs. 17240.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17600 (premium Rs. 146) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17600 (premium Rs. 116). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17862 | Lower BE: Rs. 17338
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17746 | Lower BE: Rs. 17484
C. Upper BE: Rs. 17912 | Lower BE: Rs. 17288
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17600 | Lower BE: Rs. 17338
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17862 | Lower BE: Rs. 17338
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 146 + 116 = Rs. 262. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17600 + 262 = Rs. 17862. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17600 - 262 = Rs. 17338.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17700 (premium Rs. 147) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17700 (premium Rs. 117). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17964 | Lower BE: Rs. 17436
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17847 | Lower BE: Rs. 17583
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18014 | Lower BE: Rs. 17386
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17700 | Lower BE: Rs. 17436
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 17964 | Lower BE: Rs. 17436
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 147 + 117 = Rs. 264. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17700 + 264 = Rs. 17964. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17700 - 264 = Rs. 17436.

Test 7.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17800 (premium Rs. 148) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17800 (premium Rs. 118). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18066 | Lower BE: Rs. 17534
B. Upper BE: Rs. 17948 | Lower BE: Rs. 17682
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18116 | Lower BE: Rs. 17484
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17800 | Lower BE: Rs. 17534
Test 7.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18066 | Lower BE: Rs. 17534
Test 7.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 148 + 118 = Rs. 266. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17800 + 266 = Rs. 18066. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17800 - 266 = Rs. 17534.

Test 8.question: Which is an example of a calendar spread?
options: A. Buy and sell stock together
B. Buy one-month futures and sell another month futures
C. Buy stock and short futures
D. Sell spot and buy call
Test 8.answer: B. Buy one-month futures and sell another month futures
Test 8.explanation: Calendar spread involves buying and selling futures contracts of different expiry months.

Test 8.question: An index option is a __________________.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: An index option derives its value from an underlying index like Nifty or Sensex. It is classified as a derivative product, not a debt or cash market instrument.

Test 8.question: The purchase of a share in one market and the simultaneous sale in a different market to benefit from price differentials is known as ____________.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Arbitrage means buying in one market and simultaneously selling in another to profit from price differences. It is risk-free and exploits market inefficiencies.

Test 8.question: Financial derivatives provide the facility for __________.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Derivatives serve multiple purposes — they allow trading (speculation), hedging (risk management), and arbitraging (exploiting price differences across markets).

Test 8.question: Operational risks include losses due to ____________.(NISM workbook)
options: B. Too much of management control
Test 8.explanation: Operational risk arises from inadequate internal processes, systems, or external events — such as poor disaster planning. Tax/government factors are regulatory risks.

Test 8.question: State whether TRUE or FALSE: Impact cost is low when the liquidity in the system is poor.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Impact cost is inversely related to liquidity. When liquidity is poor, buying/selling large quantities moves prices more, making impact cost HIGH, not low.

Test 8.question: Which of the following costs is not actually paid by the market participants but arises due to lack of liquidity? (NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Impact cost is an implicit cost — it is not paid directly but reflects the price movement caused by large orders in an illiquid market. STT and brokerage are explicit costs.

Test 8.question: You sold one XYZ Stock Futures contract at Rs. 278 and the lot size is 1,200. What is your profit (+) or loss (-), if you purchase the contract back at Rs. 265?(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Profit = (Sell price - Buy price) x Lot size = (278 - 265) x 1200 = 13 x 1200 = Rs. 15,600 profit (short position closed at lower price).

Test 8.question: You have taken a short position of one contract in June XYZ futures (contract multiplier 50) at a price of Rs. 3,400. When you closed this position, you made a profit of Rs. 10,000. Which closing action enabled this profit?(NISM workbook)
options: A. Selling 1 June XYZ futures contract at 3600
B. Buying 1 June XYZ futures contract at 3600
C. Buying 1 June XYZ futures contract at 3200
D. Selling 1 June XYZ futures contract at 3200
Test 8.answer: C. Buying 1 June XYZ futures contract at 3200
Test 8.explanation: Short position profits when price falls. Profit = (3400 - 3200) x 50 = 200 x 50 = Rs. 10,000. Buying back at 3200 closes the short with correct profit.

Test 8.question: A calendar spread contract in index futures attracts ___________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. Same margin as sum of two independent legs of futures contract
B. Lower margin than sum of two independent legs of futures contract
C. Higher margin than sum of two independent legs of futures contract
D. No margin need to be paid for calendar spread positions
Test 8.answer: B. Lower margin than sum of two independent legs of futures contract
Test 8.explanation: Calendar spreads have offsetting positions, so risk is lower. Hence, exchanges charge lower margin compared to two independent (unhedged) futures positions.

Test 8.question: Margins in 'Futures' trading are to be paid by _______.(NISM workbook)
options: C. Both the buyer and the seller
Test 8.answer: C. Both the buyer and the seller
Test 8.explanation: In futures trading, both buyers and sellers are obligated to pay margins. This ensures both parties honor their contractual commitments.

Test 8.question: When the near leg of the calendar spread transaction on index futures expires, the farther leg becomes a regular open position. (NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Once the near-month contract expires, the hedge is gone. The far-month contract then stands alone as an independent (regular open) position, attracting full margin.

Test 8.question: The buyer of an option cannot lose more than the option premium paid. (NISM workbook)
options: A. True only for European options
B. True only for American options
Test 8.explanation: Option buyers pay a premium upfront. Their maximum loss is always limited to this premium paid, regardless of whether the option is European or American.

Test 8.question: You sold a Put option on a share. The strike price of the put was Rs. 245 and you received a premium of Rs. 49. Theoretically, what can be the maximum loss on this position?(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Put seller's max loss occurs if stock falls to zero. Loss = Strike price - Premium received = 245 - 49 = Rs. 196. Premium received partially offsets downside.

Test 8.question: Current Price of XYZ Stock is Rs 286. Rs. 260 strike call is quoted at Rs 45. What is the Intrinsic Value?(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Intrinsic value of a call = Current Price - Strike Price = 286 - 260 = Rs. 26. The Rs. 45 premium includes intrinsic value (26) plus time value (19).

Test 8.question: A European call option gives the buyer the right but not the obligation to buy from the seller an underlying at the prevailing market price "on or before" the expiry date.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: A European option can only be exercised ON the expiry date, not before. "On or before" describes an American option. The exercise price is also the strike price, not market price.

Test 8.question: An in-the-money option is _____________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. An option with a negative intrinsic value
B. An option which cannot be profitably exercised by the holder immediately
C. An option with a positive intrinsic value
D. An option with zero-time value
Test 8.answer: C. An option with a positive intrinsic value
Test 8.explanation: An in-the-money option has positive intrinsic value — a call is ITM when market price > strike; a put is ITM when market price < strike.

Test 8.question: If an investor buys a call option with lower strike price and sells another call option with higher strike price, both on the same underlying share and same expiration date, the strategy is called ___________(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Buying a lower-strike call and selling a higher-strike call on the same expiry creates a Bull Call Spread (Bullish spread), profiting when the underlying price rises.

Test 8.question: Which of the following is a hedged position?(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: A protective put involves holding a stock and buying a put option, which hedges downside risk. Covered call provides partial hedge; short straddle/strangle are unhedged strategies.

Test 8.question: Put-call parity refers to the relationship between: ________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. Futures and options on the same stock
B. Call options on the same stock with the same maturity but different strike prices
C. Put and call options on the same stock but different strike prices and different maturity
D. Call and put options on the same stock with the same strike prices and same maturity
Test 8.answer: D. Call and put options on the same stock with the same strike prices and same maturity
Test 8.explanation: Put-call parity defines the price relationship between a call and put option on the same underlying, with identical strike price and expiration date.

Test 8.question: Which of the following situations indicates a bullish trend in the underlying?(NISM workbook)
options: A. A rising futures price along with falling open interest
B. A falling futures price along with rising open interest
C. A rising futures price along with rising open interest
D. A falling futures price along with falling open interest
Test 8.answer: C. A rising futures price along with rising open interest
Test 8.explanation: Rising futures price + rising open interest = new money entering long positions = bullish signal. It confirms upward momentum backed by fresh buying interest.

Test 8.question: On the derivative exchanges, all the orders entered on the Trading System are at prices exclusive of brokerage.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: On derivative exchanges, order prices entered on the trading system exclude brokerage. Brokerage is charged separately by the broker outside the exchange system.

Test 8.question: A trader has bought 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 780 per share. He expects the price to go up but wants to protect himself if the price falls. He does not want to lose more than Rs. 1000 on this long position in XYZ. What should the trader do?(NISM workbook)
options: A. Place a limit sell order for 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 770 per share
B. Place a stop loss sell order for 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 770 per share
C. Place a limit buy order for 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 790 per share
D. Place a limit buy order for 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 770 per share
Test 8.answer: B. Place a stop loss sell order for 100 shares of XYZ at Rs 770 per share
Test 8.explanation: A stop-loss sell order at Rs 770 triggers if price falls to 770, limiting loss to (780-770) x 100 = Rs 1,000. This protects against downside while holding the long position.

Test 8.question: Trader A wants to sell 20 contracts of August series at Rs 4500 and Trader B wants to sell 17 contracts of September series at Rs 4550. Lot size is 50. The Initial Margin is fixed at 6%. How much Initial Margin is required from both investors combined? (NISM workbook)

A member has two clients C1 and C2. C1 has purchased 800 contracts and C2 has sold 900 contracts in August XYZ futures series. What is the outstanding liability (open position) of the member towards Clearing Corporation?(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Client positions are NOT netted against each other. Open position = C1's long (800) + C2's short (900) = 1700 contracts is the gross open position reported to Clearing Corporation.

Test 8.question: Clients' positions cannot be netted off against each other while calculating initial margin on the derivatives segment. (NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Each client's position is treated independently for margin purposes. Two clients' opposite positions cannot be offset — each client must maintain their own margin separately.

Test 8.question: Mark-to-market margins are collected ___________.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: MTM margins are settled daily. At end of each trading day, gains/losses on open futures positions are calculated and paid/received by members the next morning.

Test 8.question: On the Governing Council of the Clearing Corporation of the derivatives segment, broker-members are allowed.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: To avoid conflict of interest, broker-members are NOT permitted on the Governing Council of the Clearing Corporation. Independent/public interest directors govern it.

Test 8.question: The main objective of Trade Guarantee Fund (TGF) at the exchanges is _________________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. To guarantee settlement of bonafide transactions of the members of the exchange
B. To inculcate confidence in the minds of market participants
C. To protect the interest of the investors in securities
Test 8.explanation: TGF serves all three purposes — guaranteeing settlements, building market confidence, and protecting investors. It is the exchange's safety net for trade settlements.

Test 8.question: A penalty or suspension of registration of a stock broker from derivatives exchange/segment under the SEBI (Stock Broker) Regulations, 1992 can take place if _______________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. The stock broker fails to pay fees
B. The stock broker violates the conditions of registration
C. The stock broker is suspended by the stock exchange
D. In any of the above situations
Test 8.answer: D. In any of the above situations
Test 8.explanation: SEBI can penalize or suspend a broker for any of these violations — non-payment of fees, breach of registration conditions, or exchange suspension.

Test 8.question: A defaulting member's clients' positions could be transferred to ____________ by the Clearing Corporation.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: When a member defaults, the Clearing Corporation transfers client positions to another solvent (financially sound) member to protect clients' interests and ensure continuity.

Test 8.question: Profits from derivatives transactions for Indian investors are taxed as: __________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. Speculative income under the head 'profits and gains of business or profession'
B. Short term capital gains under the head 'capital gains'
C. Non-speculative income under the head 'income from other sources'
D. Non-speculative income under the head 'profits and gains of business or profession'
Test 8.answer: A. Speculative income under the head 'profits and gains of business or profession'
Test 8.explanation: Under Indian tax law, F&O income is classified as non-speculative business income under 'profits and gains of business or profession', allowing set-off against business losses.

Test 8.question: In the case of Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs), the gains or losses arising from derivatives transactions on a recognized stock exchange are taxable as:(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: For FPIs, derivatives gains/losses on recognized exchanges are treated as short-term capital gains, unlike domestic investors for whom it is business income.

Test 8.question: When establishing a relationship with a new client, the trading member takes reasonable steps to assess the background, genuineness, beneficial identify, financial soundness of such person and his investment/trading objectives.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: This is part of the KYC (Know Your Customer) process. Trading members are required to verify client identity, financial background, and objectives before onboarding.

Test 8.question: In the KYC process, Politically Exposed Persons are termed as:(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: Under KYC/AML regulations, Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) — those holding prominent public positions — fall under 'Clients of Special Categories' requiring enhanced due diligence.

Test 8.question: _________ is allotted on client onboarding and serves as an exclusive identification of the client.(NISM workbook)
Test 8.explanation: A Unique Client Code (UCC) is assigned to every client at onboarding. It exclusively identifies the client across all transactions on the exchange.

Test 8.question: Under the Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Combating of Financial Terrorism (CFT) regulations, suspicious transactions must be reported to ___________.(NISM workbook)
options: A. Securities and Exchange Board of India
Test 8.explanation: Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) must be filed with the Financial Intelligence Unit - India (FIU-IND), the national agency that collects and analyses financial intelligence.

Test 8.question: SCORES is: __________.(NISM workbook)
options: B. Collateral Reporting System of Clearing Corporation
C. SEBI's web-based complaints redressal system
D. Customer Due Diligence and e-KYC system
Test 8.answer: C. SEBI's web-based complaints redressal system
Test 8.explanation: SCORES (SEBI Complaints Redress System) is SEBI's online platform for investors to lodge complaints against listed companies or registered intermediaries and track resolution.

Test 8.question: Who benefits when the impact cost in a stock transaction is very high?
Test 8.explanation: Impact cost is the extra cost due to low liquidity. A high impact cost increases the buying price and reduces the selling price. Therefore, both buyers and sellers are negatively affected.

Test 8.question: Initial margin in derivatives is generally higher when ______.
Test 8.explanation: When volatility is high, prices fluctuate more. This increases risk. Therefore, exchanges collect higher initial margins to protect against possible losses.

Test 8.question: Which Greek measures the change in option premium for a one unit change in volatility?
Test 8.explanation: Vega shows how much the option price changes when volatility changes. Higher volatility usually increases option premium.

Test 8.question: In a derivatives exchange, clearing members must maintain higher net worth than non-clearing members.
Test 8.explanation: Clearing members handle settlement risk and guarantee trades. Hence, they are required to maintain higher net worth.

Test 8.question: Which risk cannot be eliminated by diversification?
Test 8.explanation: Systematic risk affects the entire market (like inflation, policy changes). It cannot be removed by diversification.

Test 8.question: Can a long position in a Put option be squared off by taking a short position in a Call option with the same strike price and expiry?
Test 8.explanation: A long Put can only be closed by selling the same Put contract. A Call option is a different contract and cannot offset the Put.

Test 8.question: If the price of the underlying asset rises after entering a futures contract, what happens to the long position?
Test 8.explanation: A long futures position benefits when price rises. Profit = Selling Price – Buying Price.

Test 8.question: Is it possible to earn high returns without taking any risk?
Test 8.explanation: Higher returns always come with higher risk. No investment gives guaranteed high returns without risk.

Test 8.question: What is the approximate Delta of a far out-of-the-money option?
Test 8.explanation: Out-of-the-money options have very little chance of expiring in profit. Hence, their Delta is close to zero.

Test 8.question: What does "Mark to Market" mean in derivatives trading?
options: C. Daily revaluation of open positions
Test 8.answer: C. Daily revaluation of open positions
Test 8.explanation: Mark to Market (MTM) means daily calculation of profit or loss based on market price changes. Losses are collected and profits are credited daily.

Test 8.question: The margining system for index futures is based on ______.
Test 8.explanation: As per regulatory guidelines, margins for index futures are calculated using the Value at Risk (VaR) method. VaR estimates the maximum expected loss at a given confidence level.

Test 8.question: Mr. Ashu bought 100 shares at Rs 980 each. He does not want to lose more than Rs 1,000. What should he do?
options: A. Place stop loss at Rs 990
B. Place stop loss at Rs 970
C. Place limit buy at Rs 990
D. Place limit sell at Rs 970
Test 8.answer: B. Place stop loss at Rs 970
Test 8.explanation: Maximum Loss Allowed = Rs 1,000 Shares = 100 Loss per Share = 1000 ÷ 100 = Rs 10 Stop Loss Price = 980 – 10 = Rs 970 If price falls to Rs 970, loss per share is Rs 10. Total loss = 10 × 100 = Rs 1,000.

Test 8.question: A call option gives the buyer the right to buy the underlying asset at market price.
Test 8.explanation: A call option gives the right to buy at the strike price, not the market price. The strike price is fixed in the contract.

Test 8.question: A trader buys a futures contract at Rs 768. Lot size is 1200. He squares off at Rs 778. What is his profit or loss?
Test 8.explanation: Profit per unit = 778 – 768 = Rs 10 Total Profit = 10 × 1200 = Rs 12,000. Since price increased, the buyer makes profit of Rs 12,000.

Test 8.question: A Professional Clearing Member in derivatives segment ______.
options: A. Must also be cash segment member
B. Must join cash segment within 2 years
C. Provides trading facility to clients
Test 8.explanation: A Professional Clearing Member only clears and settles trades. They do not have rights to trade on the exchange.

Test 8.question: Theta measures sensitivity of option price to change in volatility.
Test 8.explanation: Theta measures time decay (change in price due to passage of time). Vega measures sensitivity to volatility.

Test 8.question: Which complaint can an Exchange resolve against a trading member?
options: A. Land dispute between client and broker
D. Unauthorized trade in client account
Test 8.answer: D. Unauthorized trade in client account
Test 8.explanation: Exchanges can resolve complaints related to trading issues like unauthorized transactions in a client’s account.

Test 8.question: Clearing and settlement of derivatives trades in India is done through ______.
Test 8.explanation: All derivatives trades are cleared and settled through SEBI-approved Clearing Corporations.

Test 8.question: Delta measures change in option price due to change in interest rate.
Test 8.explanation: Rho measures sensitivity to interest rate. Delta measures change in option price due to change in underlying asset price.

Test 8.question: Can a Clearing Member provide fixed deposits as part of liquid assets to Clearing Corporation?
Test 8.explanation: Fixed deposits are accepted as liquid assets along with cash, government securities, and bank guarantees for margin requirements.

Test 8.question: Vega measures change in Delta due to change in price of underlying asset.
Test 8.explanation: Vega measures sensitivity of option price to volatility. Gamma measures change in Delta with respect to change in underlying price.

Test 8.question: A short position in a Put option can be closed by taking a long position in the same Put option (same strike and expiry).
Test 8.explanation: A short Put can be squared off by buying the same Put contract with identical strike price and expiry date.

Test 8.question: On final settlement of an option, the favorable difference received by the buyer is recorded as ______ in Profit & Loss Account.
Test 8.explanation: If the option is exercised profitably, the difference received is treated as income in the Profit & Loss account.

Test 8.question: Higher the strike price, lower will be the premium of a Call option.
Test 8.explanation: As strike price increases, the intrinsic value of the call decreases. Therefore, the premium generally becomes lower.

Test 8.question: Trading in Indian equity derivatives is allowed in ______.
Test 8.explanation: Indian equity derivatives market allows trading in index options, stock options, and stock futures.

Test 8.question: Can the exercise price of an option be above, below, or equal to the current spot price?
Test 8.explanation: Strike price is fixed in the contract, while spot price changes. So, strike price can be higher, lower, or equal to spot price.

Test 8.question: Can a person sell futures contracts without owning the underlying asset?
Test 8.explanation: Futures trading does not require ownership of the underlying asset. Only margin payment is required.

Test 8.question: If three futures contracts of 1 month, 2 months, and 3 months are available, how many calendar spreads can be created?
Test 8.explanation: Possible combinations: 1 & 2, 2 & 3, 1 & 3 Total = 3 spreads Calendar spread involves contracts of different expiry months. Three combinations are possible.

Test 8.question: All 50 stocks in NIFTY 50 index are equally weighted.
Test 8.explanation: NIFTY 50 uses free-float market capitalization method. Stocks are weighted based on market cap, not equally.

Test 8.question: A Call option gives the buyer ______.
Test 8.explanation: A Call option gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy the underlying asset at the strike price.

Test 8.question: Speculators take risk while hedgers try to reduce risk.
Test 8.explanation: Hedgers use derivatives to reduce or transfer risk. Speculators accept risk in expectation of profit from price movements.

Test 8.question: Money and securities deposited by clients can be used to meet broker’s proprietary obligations.
Test 8.explanation: Client funds and securities must be kept separate. They cannot be used to meet the broker’s personal or proprietary obligations.

Test 8.question: If a stock that is part of an index undergoes a stock split, what happens to the index value?
Test 8.explanation: Stock split increases number of shares but reduces price proportionally. Total market capitalization remains same, so index value is unaffected.

Test 8.question: Time decay is beneficial to the ______.
Test 8.explanation: As time passes, option premium reduces due to time decay. This benefits the option seller because he has already received the premium.

Test 8.question: Identify the correct statement about a Put option.
options: A. Buyer has obligation to sell
B. Seller has right to buy
C. Buyer has right but not obligation to sell
Test 8.answer: C. Buyer has right but not obligation to sell
Test 8.explanation: A Put option gives the buyer the right (not obligation) to sell the underlying asset at a fixed strike price.

Test 8.question: Mr. Menon bought a futures contract and price rises. What will happen?
Test 8.explanation: In futures, profit = Selling Price – Buying Price. If price rises after buying, the buyer gains.

Test 8.question: When a forward contract is used for hedging, the premium or discount is ______.
options: B. Fully charged to P&L immediately
C. Amortized over life of contract
Test 8.answer: C. Amortized over life of contract
Test 8.explanation: As per accounting standards, forward premium or discount for hedging is spread (amortized) over the contract period.

Test 8.question: Derivatives market transfers risk from ______.
Test 8.explanation: Hedgers want to reduce risk. Speculators are willing to take risk for profit. So risk moves from hedgers to speculators.

Test 8.question: Which of the following is considered as the maximum loss limit for an option buyer?
Test 8.explanation: The maximum loss for an option buyer is limited strictly to the premium paid to acquire the option contract.

Test 8.question: To sell in futures market, a person ______.
Test 8.explanation: In futures market, ownership of underlying asset is not required. Only margin payment is necessary.

Test 8.question: The derivatives market helps in ______.
options: A. Transfer of risk to those with higher risk appetite
B. Reallocation of risk among participants
Test 8.explanation: Derivatives shift risk from hedgers (low risk appetite) to speculators (high risk appetite) and help in better risk distribution in the market.

Test 8.question: Identify the FALSE statement about options.
options: A. Options are not symmetrical in rights and obligations
B. Buyer has right, seller has obligation
Test 8.explanation: Options have non-linear payoff. Profit or loss does not move in straight line like futures. Buyer has limited loss and unlimited profit (in call).

Test 8.question: What does Market Wide Position Limit (MWPL) signify in the equity derivatives segment?
options: Maximum number of contracts an individual client can hold
Total number of shares outstanding in the cash market
Maximum open interest allowed across all members and clients for a specific underlying
The daily volume limit for a specific stock
Test 8.answer: Maximum open interest allowed across all members and clients for a specific underlying
Test 8.explanation: MWPL represents the maximum open interest allowed for a particular underlying stock across all brokers and clients combined, generally set at 20% of the non-promoter holding.

Test 8.question: If a trading member reaches prescribed position limits, he will ______.
Test 8.explanation: When position limits are reached, fresh positions cannot be opened. However, existing positions can be reduced or reversed.

Test 8.question: If the near-month contract in a calendar spread expires, the far-month contract becomes a ______.
Test 8.explanation: When one leg of spread expires, only one contract remains. This becomes a naked (open) position.

Test 8.question: A clearing member can close out transactions of a trading member if ______.
options: B. Daily settlement dues not paid
Test 8.explanation: Failure to pay margin or settlement dues is a serious violation. Clearing Corporation can close positions to control risk.

Test 8.question: Which contract is settled bilaterally (not through exchange)?
Test 8.explanation: Forward contracts are private agreements between two parties and are settled bilaterally, not through an exchange.

Test 8.question: If volatility of underlying decreases, the premium of a Call option will ______.
Test 8.explanation: Higher volatility increases option premium. Lower volatility reduces option premium.

Test 8.question: A European Call option can be exercised ______.
Test 8.explanation: European options can be exercised only on the expiry date. In India, stock and index options follow European style.

Test 8.question: Under the physical settlement framework for equity derivatives, what is the obligation of a seller of a Call option that expires In-The-Money (ITM)?
options: Roll over the position to the next month
Test 8.explanation: Under physical settlement, a Call option seller who is assigned must deliver the underlying shares to the buyer at the predetermined strike price.

Test 8.question: A financial agreement that tracks its price based on the value of a different asset is known as a:
Test 8.explanation: Derivatives do not have their own value; they get their price from another asset like shares or gold.

Test 8.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid base for a derivative contract?
options: C. A company's secret strategy meeting
Test 8.answer: C. A company's secret strategy meeting
Test 8.explanation: Derivatives must be based on tradeable assets or measurable financial variables, not private information.

Test 8.question: Historically, during which century were future-delivery deals first seen at European trade fairs?
Test 8.explanation: The sources mention that contracts for goods to be delivered later were signed at fairs in the 12th Century.

Test 8.question: In the 13th Century, how far into the future did English Monasteries sell their wool using contracts?
Test 8.explanation: Monasteries in England used to pre-sell their wool as far as 20 years in advance.

Test 8.question: What was the famous financial "bubble" involving flower futures in Holland during the 1630s?
Test 8.explanation: Between 1634 and 1637, Holland experienced a speculative boom and bust involving tulip futures.

Test 8.question: Where was the first organized rice futures market created to protect farmers from weather risks?
Test 8.explanation: Japan developed a rice futures market in the late 17th Century to help farmers manage risks from weather or war.

Test 8.question: Which exchange was founded in 1848 to bring standard rules to grain trading?
Test 8.explanation: The CBOT was created in 1848 to standardize forward contracts for commodities like grain.

Test 8.question: In what year did the first "futures contract" get officially listed on an exchange in the USA?
Test 8.explanation: While CBOT started in 1848, the first standardized "futures" contract was listed in 1865.

Test 8.question: The Chicago Butter and Egg Board eventually became known by what modern name?
Test 8.explanation: This board was reorganized in 1919 and eventually became the CME.

Test 8.question: What type of futures did the CME launch in 1972 under the International Monetary Market?
Test 8.explanation: The IMM was established in 1972 specifically for trading currency futures.

Test 8.question: Which organization launched the first marketplace specifically for trading listed options in 1973?
Test 8.explanation: The Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) was the first to offer listed options trading.

Test 8.question: [Ref. Question] A trader creates a Long Straddle by buying a Call option at strike Rs. 17900 (premium Rs. 149) and a Put option at the same strike Rs. 17900 (premium Rs. 119). Calculate the upper and lower break-even points of this strategy.
options: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18168 | Lower BE: Rs. 17632
B. Upper BE: Rs. 18049 | Lower BE: Rs. 17781
C. Upper BE: Rs. 18218 | Lower BE: Rs. 17582
D. Upper BE: Rs. 17900 | Lower BE: Rs. 17632
Test 8.answer: A. Upper BE: Rs. 18168 | Lower BE: Rs. 17632
Test 8.explanation: Total Premium Paid = Call Premium + Put Premium = 149 + 119 = Rs. 268. Upper Break-even = Strike + Total Premium = 17900 + 268 = Rs. 18168. Lower Break-even = Strike - Total Premium = 17900 - 268 = Rs. 17632.

Test 9.question: An investor holds an equity portfolio valued at Rs. 7,000,000 with a beta of 0.9. The Nifty Index is currently trading at 18200. If the lot size of Nifty futures is 50, how many contracts of Nifty futures must the investor short to hedge the portfolio against systematic risk?
Test 9.explanation: Number of contracts = (Portfolio Value * Beta) / (Nifty Futures Price * Lot Size) = (7000000 * 0.9) / (18200 * 50) = 7 contracts.

Test 9.question: Calculate the fair value of a stock futures contract if the spot price of the stock is Rs. 550, the risk-free interest rate is 6.5% per annum, the time to expiration is 35 days, and the expected dividend to be paid before expiry is Rs. 2.50 per share.
Test 9.explanation: Fair Value = Spot Price + Interest Cost - Dividend. Interest Cost = Rs. 550 * 0.0650 * 35/365 = Rs. 3.43. Fair Price = 550 + 3.43 - 2.50 = Rs. 550.93.

Test 9.question: A trader buys 1 contract of Nifty futures (lot size 50) at Rs. 15,100. The closing price on Day 1 is Rs. 14,960 and the closing price on Day 2 is Rs. 15,195. What is the Mark-to-Market (MTM) margin impact on Day 1 and Day 2?
Test 9.explanation: MTM Day 1 = (Day 1 Close - Buy Price) * Lot Size = (14960 - 15100) * 50 = Rs. -7,000. MTM Day 2 = (Day 2 Close - Day 1 Close) * Lot Size = (15195 - 14960) * 50 = Rs. 11,750.

Test 9.question: An investor constructs a Bull Call Spread by buying a Call Option at strike Rs. 17200 for a premium of Rs. 210 and selling a Call Option at strike Rs. 17600 for a premium of Rs. 85. Calculate the maximum loss, maximum profit, and the break-even point for this strategy.
options: A) Max Loss: Rs. 125 | Max Profit: Rs. 275 | Break-even: Rs. 17325
B) Max Loss: Rs. 210 | Max Profit: Rs. 400 | Break-even: Rs. 17200
C) Max Loss: Rs. 125 | Max Profit: Unlimited | Break-even: Rs. 17600
D) Max Loss: Rs. 85 | Max Profit: Rs. 325 | Break-even: Rs. 17305
Test 9.answer: A) Max Loss: Rs. 125 | Max Profit: Rs. 275 | Break-even: Rs. 17325
Test 9.explanation: Net Cost (Max Loss) = Premium Paid - Premium Received = 210 - 85 = Rs. 125. Max Profit = (Difference in Strike Prices) - Net Debit = (17600 - 17200) - 125 = Rs. 275. Break-even = Lower Strike + Net Debit = 17200 + 125 = Rs. 17325.
